Title: LIKE EARLY STARVATION 1 and EARLY STARVATION 1 promote and stabilize amylopectin phase transition in starch biosynthesis

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Col-0 ecotype; engineered yeast lines 28 and 29
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Starch-bound protein with highly conserved Trp-rich C-terminal carbohydrate-binding β-sheet domain; predicted chloroplast transit peptide; additional conserved N-terminal α-helical regions
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast starch granule; starch-bound; chloroplast transit peptide predicted
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Starch (amylopectin-rich insoluble glucan); phytoglycogen/soluble glucan as contrasted state
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf chloroplast starch granules; engineered yeast cytoplasmic glucan particles
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major plant carbohydrate reserve and carbon storage polymer
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes phase transition of amylopectin-like glucans into insoluble semi-crystalline starch; increases starch relative to phytoglycogen and promotes de novo starch granule formation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis; overexpression in Arabidopsis; heterologous expression in engineered yeast; glucan fractionation and quantification; TEM/SEM/LM imaging; SAXS; confocal localization; transient expression of truncations in Nicotiana benthamiana
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CWP2 promoter (constitutive) in yeast; overexpression as LESV-YFP in Arabidopsis line LESV-OX#4-6
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Standard 12 h light/12 h dark cycles; prolonged night destarching treatment; isoamylase-deficient background (isa1isa2); galactose-inducing growth for yeast platform
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Protein structural modeling using AlphaFold entry Q5EAH9; ortholog sequence alignment/bioinformatic conservation analysis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LESV expression in engineered yeast increased the insoluble glucan fraction and produced larger, smoother, more regular particles without majorly altering glucan chain-length distribution. 2. Arabidopsis lesv mutants showed strongly aberrant glucan formation after complete destarching, accumulating less total glucan and much more soluble glucan, indicating LESV is important for de novo starch granule initiation/formation. 3. LESV overexpression in isa1isa2 shifted carbon from phytoglycogen toward starch, whereas isa1isa2lesv accumulated phytoglycogen in all plastid types, supporting a role in promoting amylopectin phase transition.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: LESV overexpression generated numerous small starch granules and previously reported low amylose staining intensity; no major starch excess under normal conditions
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper proposes LESV acts as a nucleator/template that aligns glucan double helices through its Trp-rich surface; likely becomes encapsulated within starch granules; effect is stronger than ESV1 for promoting insoluble glucan formation; not an enzyme or TF
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to improve robust starch granule formation and carbon storage, especially under suboptimal biosynthetic backgrounds or conditions; may be useful for engineering starch crystallinity and granule properties in crops

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Col-0 ecotype; engineered yeast lines 28 and 29
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Starch-bound protein with highly conserved Trp-rich C-terminal carbohydrate-binding β-sheet domain; predicted disordered/proline-rich N-terminus; chloroplast-targeted
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast starch granule, likely granule surface-associated; starch-bound
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Starch (amylopectin-rich insoluble glucan); phytoglycogen/soluble glucan as contrasted state
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf chloroplast starch granules; engineered yeast cytoplasmic glucan particles
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major plant carbohydrate reserve and carbon storage polymer
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Stabilizes semi-crystalline starch/amylopectin and limits degradation, thereby increasing starch retention and modestly promoting insoluble glucan accumulation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis; overexpression in Arabidopsis; heterologous expression in engineered yeast; glucan fractionation and quantification; TEM/SEM/LM imaging; SAXS; confocal localization; recombinant protein structural/biophysical analyses
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CWP2 promoter (constitutive) in yeast; overexpression as ESV1-YFP in Arabidopsis line ESV1-OX#3-2
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Standard 12 h light/12 h dark cycles; prolonged night destarching treatment; isoamylase-deficient background (isa1isa2); galactose-inducing growth for yeast platform
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Protein structural modeling using AlphaFold entry F4I9G2; ortholog sequence alignment/bioinformatic conservation analysis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ESV1 expression in engineered yeast increased insoluble glucan accumulation and altered particle morphology, but its effect was weaker than LESV. 2. In Arabidopsis, ESV1 overexpression increased starch retention, especially at night, while esv1 mutants degraded starch prematurely, indicating a protective/stabilizing role. 3. In isa1isa2 backgrounds, ESV1 increased starch abundance mainly by limiting starch degradation rather than by converting phytoglycogen into starch.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression caused excess starch retention and reduced turnover; may interfere with normal starch mobilization at night
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper proposes ESV1 stabilizes exposed glucan helices at vulnerable lamellar/granule surfaces, restricting hydrolytic access; likely less effective than LESV in nucleating crystallization; not an enzyme or TF
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering target to increase starch retention or alter granule stability, but caution is needed because excessive stabilization may impair remobilization
